revision.comment: initial text of a start article -- 1st steps in a constructive direction
revision.text:
The {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|''Kamo-shi''}} are a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[sacerdotal]] kin group<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86&lpg=PA86&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.]</ref> which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.<ref>Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.]</ref> The clan rose to prominence during the [[Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-cenutry founding of the [[Kamo Shrine]].<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.]</ref>
==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref> The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.<ref name="yomiuri1">Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated [[jinja|''jinja'']] or shrines -- the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various [[kami|''kami'']] or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.<ref>Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine].</ref> Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once [[Tadasu no mori|''Tadasu no Mori'']] (糺の森),<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.]</ref> the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.<ref>Nelson, p. [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA67&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a pp. 67-69.]</ref>
==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697-1769)
Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Matsudaira before 1566, his appointment as shogun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,<ref>Nussbaum, [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.]</ref> some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."<ref>Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA158&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&lr= "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context,'' p. 158.]</ref>
==Note== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. 10-ISBN 2-706-81632-5; 13-ISBN 978-2-706-81632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82259-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82259-0 * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&dq=Japan+Encyclopedia&source=gbs_book_other_versions_r&cad=0_2 ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-00770-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-00770-3 (cloth) -- 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5 (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=sakai+tadayuki&lr=&source=gbs_summary_s&cad=0 "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context.''] London: [[Routledge]]. 10-ISBN 1-873-41003-4; 13-ISBN 978-1-873-41042-4 (cloth)

(1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.]</ref> the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.<ref>Nelson, p. Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Matsudaira before 1566, his appointment as shogun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,<ref>Nussbaum, [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.]</ref> some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."<ref>Plutschow, Herbert. [[ja:賀茂氏]]

The {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|''Kamo-shi''}} are a [[Japan|Japanese]] [[sacerdotal]] kin group<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86&lpg=PA86&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.]</ref> which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.<ref>Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.]</ref> The clan rose to prominence during the [[Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-cenutry founding of the [[Kamo Shrine]].<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.]</ref>
==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref> The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.<ref name="yomiuri1">Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[jinja]]'' or shrines -- the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.<ref>Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine].</ref>
[[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vist unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once [[Tadasu no mori|''Tadasu no Mori'']] (糺の森),<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.]</ref> the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.<ref>Nelson, p. [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA67&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a pp. 67-69.]</ref>
==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697-1769)
Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Matsudaira before 1566, his appointment as shogun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,<ref>Nussbaum, [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.]</ref> some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."<ref>Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA158&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&lr= "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context,'' p. 158.]</ref>
==Note== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * Iwao, Seiichi, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. 10-ISBN 2-7068-1632-5; 13-ISBN 978-2-7068-1632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-8248-2259-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2259-0 * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&dq=Japan+Encyclopedia&source=gbs_book_other_versions_r&cad=0_2 ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-00770-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-00770-3 (cloth) -- 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5 (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=sakai+tadayuki&lr=&source=gbs_summary_s&cad=0 "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context.''] London: [[Routledge]]. 10-ISBN 1-873410-03-4; 13-ISBN 978-1-873410-42-4 (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[Houghton Mifflin]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2832259 OCLC 2832259]

revision.comment: /* References */ links -- John Breen, Mark Teeuwen & Seiichi Iwao
revision.text:
The {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|''Kamo-shi''}} are a [[Japan]]ese [[sacerdotal]] kin group<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86&lpg=PA86&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.]</ref> which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.<ref>Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.]</ref> The clan rose to prominence during the [[Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo Shrine]].<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.]</ref>
==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref> The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.<ref name="yomiuri1">Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.<ref>Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine].</ref>
[[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once [[Tadasu no mori|''Tadasu no Mori'']] (糺の森),<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.]</ref> the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.<ref>Nelson, p. [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA67&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a pp. 67-69.]</ref>
==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769)
Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Matsudaira before 1566, his appointment as shogun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,<ref>Nussbaum, [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.]</ref> some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."<ref>Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA158&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&lr= "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context,'' p. 158.]</ref>
==Note== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * [[Breen, John (scholar)|Breen, John and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. 10-ISBN 2-7068-1632-5; 13-ISBN 978-2-7068-1632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-8248-2259-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2259-0 * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&dq=Japan+Encyclopedia&source=gbs_book_other_versions_r&cad=0_2 ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-00770-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-00770-3 (cloth) -- 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5 (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=sakai+tadayuki&lr=&source=gbs_summary_s&cad=0 "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context.''] London: [[Routledge]]. 10-ISBN 1-873410-03-4; 13-ISBN 978-1-873410-42-4 (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[Houghton Mifflin]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2832259 OCLC 2832259]

(2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.]</ref>

(1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.]</ref> the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.<ref>Nelson, p. [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA67&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a pp. 67-69.]</ref>

Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Matsudaira before 1566, his appointment as shogun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,<ref>Nussbaum, [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.]</ref> some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."<ref>Plutschow, Herbert. revision.comment: Bot: [[Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Layout#Standard appendices and footers|standard sections headers]] and minor changes
revision.text:
The {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|''Kamo-shi''}} are a [[Japan]]ese [[sacerdotal]] kin group<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86&lpg=PA86&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.]</ref> which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.<ref>Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.]</ref> The clan rose to prominence during the [[Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo Shrine]].<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.]</ref>
==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref> The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.<ref name="yomiuri1">Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.<ref>Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine].</ref>
[[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once [[Tadasu no mori|''Tadasu no Mori'']] (糺の森),<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.]</ref> the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.<ref>Nelson, p. [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA67&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a pp. 67-69.]</ref>
==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769)
Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Matsudaira before 1566, his appointment as shogun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,<ref>Nussbaum, [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.]</ref> some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."<ref>Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA158&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&lr= "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context,'' p. 158.]</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. 10-ISBN 2-7068-1632-5; 13-ISBN 978-2-7068-1632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-8248-2259-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2259-0 * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&dq=Japan+Encyclopedia&source=gbs_book_other_versions_r&cad=0_2 ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-674-00770-0; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-00770-3 (cloth) -- 10-ISBN 0-674-01753-6; 13-ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5 (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=sakai+tadayuki&lr=&source=gbs_summary_s&cad=0 "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context.''] London: [[Routledge]]. 10-ISBN 1-873410-03-4; 13-ISBN 978-1-873410-42-4 (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[Houghton Mifflin]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2832259 OCLC 2832259]

[http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.]</ref> the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.<ref>Nelson, p. [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA67&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a pp. 67-69.]</ref>
==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769)
Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Matsudaira before 1566, his appointment as shogun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,<ref>Nussbaum, [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.]</ref> some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."<ref>Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA158&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&lr= "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context,'' p. 158.]</ref>

[http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. 10-ISBN 2-7068-1632-5; 13-ISBN 978-2-7068-1632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-8248-2259-5; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2259-0 * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. revision.text:
{{Japanese name|Kamo}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a [[Japan]]ese [[sacerdotal]] kin group<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86&lpg=PA86&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.]</ref> which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.<ref>Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.]</ref> The clan rose to prominence during the [[Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo Shrine]].<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.]</ref>
==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref> The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.<ref name="yomiuri1">Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.<ref>Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine].</ref>
[[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [http://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.]</ref> the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.<ref>Nelson, p. [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA67&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a pp. 67-69.]</ref>
==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769)
Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Matsudaira before 1566, his appointment as shogun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,<ref>Nussbaum, [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.]</ref> some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."<ref>Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA158&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&lr= "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context,'' p. 158.]</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. ISBN 978-2-7068-1632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. ISBN 978-0-8248-2259-0 * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [http://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&dq=Japan+Encyclopedia&source=gbs_book_other_versions_r&cad=0_2 ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. ISBN 978-0-674-00770-3 (cloth) -- ISBN 978-0-674-01753-5 (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [http://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=sakai+tadayuki&lr=&source=gbs_summary_s&cad=0 "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context.''] London: [[Routledge]]. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/2832259 OCLC 2832259]

The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.<ref>Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine].</ref>
[[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.]</ref> the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.<ref>Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA67&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a pp. 67-69.]</ref>
==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769)
Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Matsudaira before 1566, his appointment as shogun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,<ref>Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.]</ref> some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."<ref>Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA158&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&lr= "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context,'' p. 158.]</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. ISBN 978-2-7068-1632-1; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. ISBN 978-0-8248-2259-0 * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&dq=Japan+Encyclopedia&source=gbs_book_other_versions_r&cad=0_2 ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. revision.text:
{{Japanese name|Kamo}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a [[Japan]]ese [[sacerdotal]] kin group<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86&lpg=PA86&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.]</ref> which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.<ref>Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.]</ref> The clan rose to prominence during the [[Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo Shrine]].<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.]</ref>
==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref> The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.<ref name="yomiuri1">Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.<ref>Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine].</ref>
[[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.]</ref> the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.<ref>Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA67&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a pp. 67-69.]</ref>
==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769)
Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Matsudaira before 1566, his appointment as shogun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,<ref>Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.]</ref> some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."<ref>Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA158&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&lr= "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context,'' p. 158.]</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&dq=Japan+Encyclopedia&source=gbs_book_other_versions_r&cad=0_2 ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a [[Japan]]ese [[sacerdotal]] kin group<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86&lpg=PA86&dq= ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.]</ref> which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.<ref>Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.]</ref> The clan rose to prominence during the [[Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo Shrine]].<ref>Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.]</ref>
==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.<ref>Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA92&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.]</ref> The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.<ref name="yomiuri1">Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005.</ref>
The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.<ref>Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}.</ref>
[[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.]</ref> the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.<ref>Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA67&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a pp. 67-69.]</ref>
==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769)
Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,<ref>Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.]</ref> some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."<ref>Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&pg=PA158&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&lr= "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context,'' p. 158.]</ref>
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC&client=firefox-a ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+and+branch&lr=&as_drrb_is=q&as_minm_is=0&as_miny_is=&as_maxm_is=0&as_maxy_is=&as_brr=0&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&dq=Japan+Encyclopedia&source=gbs_book_other_versions_r&cad=0_2 ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=sakai+tadayuki&lr=&source=gbs_summary_s&cad=0 "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context.''] London: [[Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}}
==External links==
[[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Meiji Restoration]]

(2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586&lpg=PA206&dq= ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.]</ref>

[[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),<ref>Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&pg=PA479&dq=tadasu+no+mori&lr=&client=firefox-a ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.]</ref> the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.<ref>Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&pg=PA67&dq=kamo+clan&client=firefox-a pp. 67-69.]</ref>
==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769)

{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro]] | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 "Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context,'' p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. {{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro]] | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: [[Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Meiji Restoration]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Test]] {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}}

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] to [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro]] | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: [[Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Meiji Restoration]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}}

[https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: [[Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Meiji Restoration]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia37]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia37]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia37]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia37]] [[Category:dd]] {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}}

{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro]] | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: [[Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Meiji Restoration]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia37]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia37]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia37]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia37]] {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}}

Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. {{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro]] | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: [[Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Meiji Restoration]] [[Category:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}}
