revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro]] | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: [[Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[sn:Kamo clan]] [[en:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

London: [[Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[sn:Kamo clan]] [[en:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo clan]][[Category:qq]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro]] | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: [[Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[sn:Kamo clan]] [[en:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo clan]][[Category:qq]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = [[Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro]] | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[:en:Kamo no Chōmei|Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: [[Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[sn:Kamo clan]] [[en:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kamo clan]][[Category:qq]]

The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[:en:Kamo no Chōmei|Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: [[Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[sn:Kamo clan]] [[en:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[sn:Kamo clan]]

(2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[:en:Kamo no Chōmei|Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro]] | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[:en:Sacerdotal|Sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[:en:Kamo no Chōmei|Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: [[:en:Routledge|Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[sn:Kamo clan]] [[en:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[sn:Kamo clan]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: [[:en:Routledge|Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[sn:Kamo clan]] [[en:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[sn:Kamo clan]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2975287]] Wikidata name: Kamo clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2975287]] Wikidata name: Kamo clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro]] | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[:en:Sacerdotal|Sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[:en:Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[:en:Kamo no Chōmei|Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: [[:en:Routledge|Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[sn:Kamo clan]] [[en:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[sn:Kamo clan]]

(2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[:en:Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[:en:Kamo no Chōmei|Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2975287]] Wikidata name: Kamo clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2975287]] Wikidata name: Kamo clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro]] | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[:en:Sacerdotal|Sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[:en:Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[:en:Kamo no Chōmei|Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. London: [[:en:Routledge|Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[en:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]][[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{wikidata link|Q2975287}}

[[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2975287]] Wikidata name: Kamo clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2975287]] Wikidata name: Kamo clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro]] | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[:en:Sacerdotal|Sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[:en:Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[:en:Kamo no Chōmei|Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[:en:Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[:en:Kamo no Chōmei|Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: [[:en:Routledge|Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[en:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]][[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{wikidata link|Q2975287}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[:en:Sacerdotal|Sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[:en:Kamo no Chōmei|Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2975287]] Wikidata name: Kamo clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2975287]] Wikidata name: Kamo clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro]] | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[:en:Sacerdotal|Sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[:en:Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[:en:Kamo no Chōmei|Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. London: [[:en:Routledge|Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:japanese clans]] }} [[en:Kamo clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]][[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{wikidata link|Q2975287}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]
