[[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2975287]] Wikidata name: Kamo clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2975287]] Wikidata name: Kamo clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro]] | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[:en:Sacerdotal|Sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka]] and [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''[[:en:Shinto shrine|jinja]]'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * [[:en:Kamo no Chōmei|Kamo no Chōmei]] (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the [[:en:Matsudaira clan|Matsudaira]] used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Seiichi Iwao|Iwao, Seiichi]], Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: [[:en:Routledge|Routledge]]. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Kamo clan]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{wikidata link|Q2975287}} [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} [[en:Kamo clan]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{wikidata link|Q2975287}} [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2975287]] Wikidata name: Kamo clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2975287]] Wikidata name: Kamo clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro}} | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[:en:Sacerdotal|Sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}} shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian}} periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=jinja}}'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * {{ill|Kamo no Chōmei|en|Kamo no Chōmei|WD=Q291329|lt=Kamo no Chōmei}} (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji}}. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the {{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira}} used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Seiichi Iwao|en|Seiichi Iwao|WD=Q7446707|lt=Iwao, Seiichi}}, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|WD=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: {{ill|Routledge|en|Routledge|WD=Q1508259|lt=Routledge}}. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} [[en:Kamo clan]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{wikidata link|Q2975287}} [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} [[en:Kamo clan]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{wikidata link|Q2975287}} [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

[[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro}} | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[:en:Sacerdotal|Sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}} shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian}} periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=jinja}}'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * {{ill|Kamo no Chōmei|en|Kamo no Chōmei|WD=Q291329|lt=Kamo no Chōmei}} (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji}}. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the {{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira}} used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Seiichi Iwao|en|Seiichi Iwao|WD=Q7446707|lt=Iwao, Seiichi}}, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|WD=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: {{ill|Routledge|en|Routledge|WD=Q1508259|lt=Routledge}}. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} [[en:Kamo clan]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{wikidata link|Q2975287}} [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

revision.text:
[[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] [[en:Kamo clan]][[fr:Clan Kamo]][[ja:賀茂県主氏]][[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{family name hatnote|Kamo|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | surname nihongo = 賀茂氏 | image = File:Tokugawa family crest.svg | image size = 140px | image caption = [[Mon (emblem)|Mon]] of the Kamo clan | home province = {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro|1=Yamashiro Province}} | founding year = 7th century |cadet branches=[[Miwa clan]] (possibly only in legend),
{{ill|Kadenokōji family|ja|勘解由小路家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadenokōji family|qq=}}|founder=[[Kamo no Okimi]]}} {{nihongo|'''Kamo clan'''|賀茂氏|Kamo-shi}} is a Japanese [[:en:Sacerdotal|Sacerdotal]] kin groupBreen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka|1=Asuka period}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian|1=Heian period}} periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=jinja|1=Shinto shrine}}'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * {{ill|Kamo no Chōmei|en|Kamo no Chōmei|WD=Q291329|lt=Kamo no Chōmei|1=Kamo no Chōmei}} (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}}. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the {{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|WD=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira|1=Matsudaira clan}} used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Seiichi Iwao|en|Seiichi Iwao|WD=Q7446707|lt=Iwao, Seiichi|1=Seiichi Iwao}}, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|WD=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press|1=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&q=sakai+tadayuki ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'']. London: {{ill|Routledge|en|Routledge|WD=Q1508259|lt=Routledge|1=Routledge}}. {{ISBN|978-1-873410-42-4}} (cloth) * Terry, Thomas Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&q=tadasu+no+mori ''Terry's Japanese empire: including Korea and Formosa, with chapters on Manchuria, the Trans-Siberian railway, and the chief ocean routes to Japan; a guidebook for travelers.''] New York: [[:en:Houghton Mifflin|Houghton Mifflin]]. {{OCLC|2832259}} {{Kamo Faith}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} [[en:Kamo clan]] [[fr:Clan Kamo]] [[ja:賀茂県主氏]] [[pt:Clã Kamo]] {{wikidata link|Q2975287}} [[Category:japanese clans]] [[Category:Shinbetsu]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

(2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&pg=PA86 ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' p. 86.] which traces its roots from a {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} shrine in the vicinity of northeastern Kyoto.Shimogamo-jinja web site: [http://www.shimogamo-jinja.or.jp/english/index.html history.] The clan rose to prominence during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka|1=Asuka period}} and {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian|1=Heian period}} periods when the Kamo are identified with the 7th-century founding of the [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]].Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA586 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 586.] ==Kamo Shrine== The Kamo Shrine's name references the area's early inhabitants, many of whom continue to live near the shrine their ancestors traditionally served.Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA92 ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan,'' pp. 92-99.] The formal names of corollary ''jinja'' memorialize vital clan roots in a history which pre-dates the founding of Japan's ancient capital.Miyazaki, Makoto. [http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens170.htm "Lens on Japan: Defending Heiankyo from Demons,"] ''Daily Yomiuri.'' December 20, 2005. The Kamo Shrine encompasses what are now independent but traditionally associated ''{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=jinja|1=Shinto shrine}}'' or shrines—the {{Nihongo|Kamo-wakeikazuchi Shrine|賀茂別雷神社|''Kamo-wakeikazuchi jinja''}} in Kyoto's Kita Ward and; and the {{Nihongo|"Kamo-mioya Shrine'"|賀茂御祖神社|''Kamo-mioya jinja''}} in Sakyo Ward. The ''jinja'' names identify the various ''[[kami]]'' or deities who are venerated; and the name also refers to the ambit of shrine's nearby woods.Kamigamo-jinja web site: [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html about the shrine] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221145021/http://kamigamojinja.jp/english/index-e.html |date=2009-02-21 }}. [[File:Tadasu no mori 200906a.jpg|thumb|right|A wild vista unfolds at ''Tadasu no Mori''.]] Although now incorporated within boundaries of the city, the location was once ''[[Tadasu no Mori]]'' (糺の森),Terry, Philip. (1914). [https://books.google.com/books?id=9ipbUA13CHoC&dq=tadasu+no+mori&pg=PA479 ''Terry's Japanese empire,'' p. 479.] the wild forest home of the exclusive caretakers of the shrine from prehistoric times.Nelson, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&dq=kamo+clan&pg=PA67 pp. 67-69.] ==Notable clan members== {{dynamic list}} * {{ill|Kamo no Chōmei|en|Kamo no Chōmei|qid=Q291329|lt=Kamo no Chōmei|1=Kamo no Chōmei}} (1155–1216) * [[Kamo no Mabuchi]] (1697–1769) Although [[:en:Ieyasu Tokugawa|Ieyasu Tokugawa]] never used the surname Tokugawa before 1566, his appointment as shōgun was contingent on his claim to Matsudaira kinship and a link to the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|qid=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}}. Modern scholarship has revealed that the genealogy proffered to the emperor contained falsified information; however, since the {{ill|Matsudaira clan|en|Matsudaira clan|qid=Q1141077|lt=Matsudaira|1=Matsudaira clan}} used the same crest as the Kamo clan,Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA34 ''Japan Encyclopedia,'' p. 34.] some academics suggest that he was likely a descendant of the Kamo clan."Plutschow, Herbert. (1995). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fNQjDQ-mWYgC&dq=plutschow+and+kamo&pg=PA158 ''Japan's Name Culture: The Significance of Names in a Religious, Political and Social Context'', p. 158.] ==Genealogy== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Seiichi Iwao|en|Seiichi Iwao|qid=Q7446707|lt=Iwao, Seiichi|1=Seiichi Iwao}}, Teizō Iyanaga, Susumu Ishii, Shōichirō Yoshida, ''et al.'' (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=fW9v37poqcQC ''Dictionnaire historique du Japon.''] Paris: Maisonneuve & Larose. {{ISBN|978-2-7068-1632-1}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/isbn/2706816325 OCLC 51096469] * Nelson, John K. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SPchlKNrj0UC&q=kamo+and+branch ''Enduring Identities: The Guise of Shinto in Contemporary Japan.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2259-0}} * Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric and Käthe Roth. [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|qid=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press|1=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3}} (cloth) -- {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}} (paper) * Plutschow, Herbert.
